name: nexmon

documentation.readme: What is nexmon? Nexmon is our C-based firmware patching framework for Broadcom/Cypress WiFi chips that enables you to write your own firmware patches, for example, to enable monitor mode with radiotap headers and frame injection.
usage_sections: http://nexmon.org/jammer: A real Wi-Fi jammer that allows to overlay ongoing frame transmissions with an arbitrary jamming signal.
It uses the Wi-Fi chip as a Software-defined Radio to generate jamming signals
It allows using non-standard channels such as 80 MHz bandwidth in the 2.4 GHz bands
It allows to set arbitrary transmission powers
It allows patching the D11 core's real-time MAC implementation

arguments.name: --target
arguments.description: Target host, IP, MAC, or URL the tool operates on.

arguments.name: --port
arguments.description: Target port (default: service default).

arguments.name: --interface
arguments.description: Network interface to bind to (e.g. wlan0mon, eth0).

arguments.name: --output
arguments.description: Output file path for the report or capture.

arguments.name: --verbose
arguments.description: Increase log verbosity (repeatable for higher levels).

arguments.name: --threads
arguments.description: Number of concurrent workers.

arguments.name: --timeout
arguments.description: Per-operation timeout in seconds.

arguments.name: --rate
arguments.description: Packet / request rate limit (packets/sec).

arguments.name: --wordlist
arguments.description: Path to a wordlist (users, passwords, paths).

arguments.name: --proxy
arguments.description: HTTP or SOCKS proxy URL for outbound requests.

arguments.name: --user-agent
arguments.description: HTTP User-Agent header value.

arguments.name: --cookie
arguments.description: HTTP Cookie header value.

arguments.name: --header
arguments.description: Extra HTTP header in key:value form (repeatable).

arguments.name: --insecure
arguments.description: Skip TLS certificate verification (lab use only).

arguments.name: --json
arguments.description: Emit JSON output instead of human-readable text.

arguments.name: --no-color
arguments.description: Disable ANSI color in output.

arguments.name: --quiet
arguments.description: Suppress non-error output.

arguments.name: --dry-run
arguments.description: Print what would be done without making any changes.

arguments.name: --yes
arguments.description: Skip confirmation prompts (use with care).

arguments.name: --force
arguments.description: Force the action even if a safety check would block it.

function_signatures.name: configure
function_signatures.description: Configure the tool with runtime options (interface, target, etc.) before execution.

function_signatures.name: scan
function_signatures.description: Perform a read-only scan / probe of the target.

function_signatures.name: exploit
function_signatures.description: Run the offensive step against the target. Returns ok, data, error envelope.

function_signatures.name: report
function_signatures.description: Render the result into a human-readable or machine-readable report.

function_signatures.name: validate
function_signatures.description: Pre-flight check: confirm the target is reachable and the tool can run.

function_signatures.name: cleanup
function_signatures.description: Remove any artifacts left behind by the tool (pcaps, sessions, files).

function_signatures.name: chain
function_signatures.description: Take a previous tool's result and feed it as input to this tool.

examples: loaded by the driver into the RAM. To analyze the whole firmware, one needs to extract the ROM. There are two
options to do this. Either you write a firmware patch that simply copies the contents of the ROM to RAM and then
you dump the RAM, or you directly dump the ROM after loading the regular firmware into the RAM. Even though,
By dumping the ROM after loading the original RAM firmware, it contains flash patches. Hence, the ROM needs to be
dumped again for every RAM firmware update to be consistent. As a conclusion, we prefer to dump the clean ROM after
To dump the ROM directly, you need to know, where to find it and how large it is. On chips with Cortex-M3 it is
usually at upper addresses such as 0x800000, while on chips with Cortex-R4 it is likely at 0x0. Run dhdutil to
RAM and them dumps it using dhdutil. To dump the ROM simply execute the following in the project directory:
documentation.how_to_use: Run the primary entrypoint as shown in upstream docs, e.g. `sudo dpkg --add-architecture i386`. Further invocations from upstream: `loaded by the driver into the RAM. To analyze the whole firmware, one needs to extract the ROM. There are two`; `options to do this. Either you write a firmware patch that simply copies the contents of the ROM to RAM and then`.
documentation.when_to_use: Use during the 'any' phase against surfaces [wifi_2_4_ghz, wifi_5_ghz, shell_linux] in the Wireless category. Fits when the engagement needs: ![NexMon logo](https://github.com/seemoo-lab/nexmon/raw/master/gfx/nexmon.png).
documentation.why_to_use: Prefer this tool based on upstream description: What is nexmon? Nexmon is our C-based firmware patching framework for Broadcom/Cypress WiFi chips that enables you to write your own firmware patches, for example, to enable monitor mode with radiotap headers and frame injection.
